questions.q: In the standard convention $p\le q$ in a forcing poset $\mathbb P$ means:
choices: $p$ is a weaker condition than $q$ (less information)
$p$ is a stronger condition than $q$ (more information, narrower)
questions.hint: By the Cohen/Kunen convention, $p\le q$ means $p$ extends $q$ — $p$ has more information and rules out more of the unknown object.
questions.explain: By the Cohen/Kunen convention, $p\le q$ means $p$ extends $q$ — $p$ has more information and rules out more of the unknown object. Some authors flip the order; we follow the standard 'going down means stronger'.

questions.q: A subset $D\subseteq\mathbb P$ is dense iff:
choices: Every two conditions in $D$ are comparable.
$D$ is closed under taking weaker conditions.
For every $p\in\mathbb P$ there is $q\le p$ with $q\in D$.
questions.hint: Density is exactly the property that every condition can be extended into $D$.
questions.explain: Density is exactly the property that every condition can be extended into $D$. Each dense set encodes a question we want answered; meeting it is committing to an answer.

questions.q: In Cohen forcing $2^{<\omega}$ (finite binary strings ordered by extension), how many immediate successors $q\le p$ does any condition $p$ of length $n$ have at length $n+1$?
questions.hint: Each finite string $p$ extends in two ways: append 0 or append 1.
questions.explain: Each finite string $p$ extends in two ways: append 0 or append 1. So the poset is the infinite binary tree growing downward.

questions.q: An $M$-generic filter $G\subseteq\mathbb P$ is required to:
choices: Meet every dense subset $D\subseteq\mathbb P$ (whether or not $D\in M$).
questions.hint: Genericity is relative to the ground model: $G$ must meet every dense set that $M$ can see.
questions.explain: Genericity is relative to the ground model: $G$ must meet every dense set that $M$ can see. Since $M$ is countable, only countably many dense sets need to be met, and a generic $G$ exists by a Baire-category-style diagonalization outside $M$.

questions.q: Why does the ground model $M$ usually need to be countable for the forcing construction?
choices: Because uncountable models cannot satisfy ZFC.
So that the family of dense subsets in $M$ is countable, letting us build $G$ by enumerating them.
To avoid the axiom of choice.
questions.hint: Countability of $M$ ensures only countably many dense sets in $M$, so a filter meeting all of them is built by induction along an $\omega$-enumeration.
questions.explain: Countability of $M$ ensures only countably many dense sets in $M$, so a filter meeting all of them is built by induction along an $\omega$-enumeration. Löwenheim–Skolem produces such a countable model.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes $M[G]$?
choices: The smallest transitive model of ZFC containing $M\cup\{G\}$.
questions.hint: $M[G]$ is built by interpreting $\mathbb P$-names from $M$ using $G$.
questions.explain: $M[G]$ is built by interpreting $\mathbb P$-names from $M$ using $G$. It is transitive, contains $M$ and $G$, satisfies ZFC, and is the smallest such model — every step is forced from data already in $M$.

forcing-truth-lemma.title: The forcing relation and truth lemma

questions.q: The truth lemma asserts that for any sentence $\varphi$ in the forcing language and $M$-generic $G$:
questions.hint: The truth lemma: a statement holds in $M[G]$ exactly when some condition in the generic filter forces it.
questions.explain: The truth lemma: a statement holds in $M[G]$ exactly when some condition in the generic filter forces it. The forcing relation is definable inside $M$, so $M$ controls the truth in $M[G]$ from below.

questions.q: Which of these is a key property of the forcing relation $\Vdash$?
choices: It is monotone: if $p\Vdash\varphi$ and $q\le p$ then $q\Vdash\varphi$.
It is decidable for every $p$ and $\varphi$.
It is the same as classical truth.
questions.hint: Forcing is monotone (downward): a stronger condition forces everything its predecessor forced.
questions.explain: Forcing is monotone (downward): a stronger condition forces everything its predecessor forced. It is also definable inside $M$ (a key technical theorem of Cohen), so $M$ recognises which conditions force what.

questions.q: Which fact about $\Vdash$ explains why $M[G]$ satisfies ZFC?
choices: Every ZFC axiom is forced by the maximal element $\mathbb 1\in\mathbb P$.
Forcing is a meta-theory argument that bypasses ZFC.
questions.hint: One proves in $M$ that $\mathbb 1\Vdash\sigma$ for each ZFC axiom $\sigma$; by the truth lemma each axiom holds in $M[G]$.
questions.explain: One proves in $M$ that $\mathbb 1\Vdash\sigma$ for each ZFC axiom $\sigma$; by the truth lemma each axiom holds in $M[G]$. So $M[G]\models$ ZFC for any generic $G$.

questions.q: In Cohen forcing $\mathbb P=2^{<\omega}$, the generic filter $G$ produces:
choices: A new countable ordinal not in $M$.
A single new real $r_G\in 2^\omega$, the union of all conditions in $G$.
questions.hint: $G$ is a chain of finite binary strings cofinal in length, and $r_G=\bigcup G\in 2^\omega$ is a single new infinite binary sequence — the Cohen real.
questions.explain: $G$ is a chain of finite binary strings cofinal in length, and $r_G=\bigcup G\in 2^\omega$ is a single new infinite binary sequence — the Cohen real. It is not in $M$ because its membership is decided dense-set by dense-set.

questions.q: Why is $r_G$ guaranteed not to be any specific real $s\in 2^\omega$ from $M$?
choices: Because the set $D_s=\{p\in\mathbb P: p\text{ disagrees with }s\text{ at some bit}\}$ is dense in $M$, and $G$ meets it.
Because the axiom of choice fails in $M[G]$.
questions.hint: For each $s\in (2^\omega)^M$, the set of conditions disagreeing with $s$ is dense (extend $p$ by flipping the next bit).
questions.explain: For each $s\in (2^\omega)^M$, the set of conditions disagreeing with $s$ is dense (extend $p$ by flipping the next bit). Genericity forces $r_G$ to differ from $s$ — so $r_G\ne s$ for every old real.

questions.q: The product Cohen forcing $\operatorname{Fn}(\aleph_2\times\omega,2)$ — finite partial functions — is used to:
choices: Add $\aleph_2$ many distinct generic reals while preserving cardinals.
questions.hint: Cohen's product forcing adds $\aleph_2$ mutually distinct generic reals indexed by $\aleph_2$.
questions.explain: Cohen's product forcing adds $\aleph_2$ mutually distinct generic reals indexed by $\aleph_2$. The countable chain condition (ccc) ensures no cardinal is collapsed, so $\aleph_2^M=\aleph_2^{M[G]}$ and $2^{\aleph_0}\ge\aleph_2$ in the extension.

choices: ZFC does not prove $\neg$CH (so CH is consistent).
ZFC does not prove CH (so $\neg$CH is consistent).
questions.hint: Forcing produces a model of ZFC + $\neg$CH, so $\neg$CH is consistent with ZFC; equivalently, ZFC does not prove CH.
questions.explain: Forcing produces a model of ZFC + $\neg$CH, so $\neg$CH is consistent with ZFC; equivalently, ZFC does not prove CH. Combined with Gödel's earlier consistency of CH, CH is independent of ZFC.

questions.q: Which pair of authors completes the independence proof of CH?
choices: Gödel (CH consistent via $L$) and Cohen ($\neg$CH consistent via forcing)
questions.hint: Gödel built the constructible universe $L$ in 1940 and showed $L\models$ ZFC + GCH, giving Con(ZFC + CH).
questions.explain: Gödel built the constructible universe $L$ in 1940 and showed $L\models$ ZFC + GCH, giving Con(ZFC + CH). Cohen invented forcing in 1963 to build a model of ZFC + $\neg$CH. Together: independence.

questions.q: In Cohen's original $\neg$CH model, what is the value of $2^{\aleph_0}$ as an aleph index? (Answer the subscript: $2^{\aleph_0}=\aleph_n$, give $n$.)
questions.hint: Cohen forced $\aleph_2$ many new reals while preserving cardinals, so the continuum becomes $\aleph_2$ in the extension.
questions.explain: Cohen forced $\aleph_2$ many new reals while preserving cardinals, so the continuum becomes $\aleph_2$ in the extension. Subsequent work (Solovay, Easton) shows $2^{\aleph_0}$ can be any cardinal of uncountable cofinality.

questions.q: Which of the following is independent of ZFC?
choices: The Suslin hypothesis (every ccc complete dense linear order without endpoints is order-isomorphic to $\mathbb R$).
questions.hint: Jensen showed $V=L$ implies $\neg$SH (a Suslin tree exists), while Solovay–Tennenbaum forced SH using countable-support iterated forcing.
questions.explain: Jensen showed $V=L$ implies $\neg$SH (a Suslin tree exists), while Solovay–Tennenbaum forced SH using countable-support iterated forcing. So SH is independent of ZFC.

questions.q: Martin's axiom (MA) is best described as:
choices: A weakening of CH consistent with $\neg$CH that says ccc forcings of size $<2^{\aleph_0}$ admit generic-like filters meeting any small family of dense sets.
An equivalent of the axiom of choice.
questions.hint: MA says: for any ccc poset $\mathbb P$ and family $\mathcal D$ of $<2^{\aleph_0}$ dense sets, a filter meeting all of $\mathcal D$ exists.
questions.explain: MA says: for any ccc poset $\mathbb P$ and family $\mathcal D$ of $<2^{\aleph_0}$ dense sets, a filter meeting all of $\mathcal D$ exists. Solovay–Tennenbaum (1971) forced MA + $\neg$CH; under CH it is automatic but uninformative.

choices: Every supercompact cardinal collapses under any forcing.
Supercompactness can be made indestructible under a class of further forcings via a 'Laver preparation' iteration.
ZFC is inconsistent if a supercompact exists.
questions.hint: Laver showed that after a suitable preparatory forcing, a supercompact cardinal $\kappa$ remains supercompact in any further $<\kappa$-directed-closed forcing extension.
questions.explain: Laver showed that after a suitable preparatory forcing, a supercompact cardinal $\kappa$ remains supercompact in any further $<\kappa$-directed-closed forcing extension. This is the template for many large-cardinal indestructibility results.
